speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 5, March 9, 1997. Survivor is Albert Roland, interviewer [? Tony ?] [? Catch Binstock ?]. We're interviewing in Denver, Colorado, United States of America. And the interview is being done in English. We were-- and this is tape 5. What was your cousin's name you said you had run into? Give me her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Ela Rosenblatt. But she got married, and her name was Krzetowski.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her--

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband's name was Leo. And they both survived from the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met them again in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I met them again in-- in-- in Austria, and on Bialik camp. And it was not far from Salzburg. I think it was on the outskirts of Salzburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Salzburg was a transition camp, you know, because the people that they took out that were running away from Poland or other places, you know, they were keeping them just a few days, you know, to go farther. And from there, we went to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were telling us about Windischbergerdorf.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came to Windischbergerdorf from over there. So Windischbergerdorf was a Hitler youth camp. And-- and we occupied it. UNRRA took it away and occupied, you know. It was at DP camp. And I worked. I worked as a electrician. My future father-in-law, he worked in a-- as a mechanic over there. So everybody made themselves useful. A lot of people didn't have nothing to do. So they-- they were loafing around. And I figure that it's better to work than to loaf around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did-- who did you work for then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, for the-- there was an administration there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you paid?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember, you know,. I don't remember to get paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your housing like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh?

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your housing like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wel, just like anybody else. There was big barracks. There was about 20 people with bunk beds, you know, up and down. And this is how we lived. You know, people that were married with children, they had different quarters already, probably the administration, the previous administration from the Hitler youth lived, probably they had-- because with kids, you know, they had to have more privacy. But people, like single people, and just husband and wife, they just put them in big barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were men and women together in the barracks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you get married then? You said your future wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: Windischbergerdorf we decided to get married. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So oddly enough, my father-in-law used to be a baker, you know, what they call it, from sweets, you know, from cakes and cookies, you know, years ago when he was still single. You know, he still remembered. So he started baking cakes for the wedding. And he bake good cakes. You know, you couldn't get a good cake after the war in Germany. It was not the same thing. So he made good cakes. And we made a wedding. We invited our-- all the inhabitants from the camp. We had a guy that had a rabbi's license, you know. So he was a rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he gave us [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was nice. And now I was with the family. But now I was official with the family. And that was our honeymoon in those barracks. [LAUGHS] It was really a mess. But you know,, you have to take life the way it comes, you know. You couldn't change it. But finally, we decided we're going to go. We had some friends from Stuttgart. [INAUDIBLE] another camp, you know, a DP camp. Stuttgart was already a big-- big city. You know, it was already different. And we tried always to improve our lot. In a big city, it's different life, you know. It's not like in the little-- little town, you know, and there was nothing to do over there. So we decided to go there. And we thought that from the UNRRA, you know, we can get, you know, a place in the camp. The camp over there was not a camp like we had in Windischbergerdorf, though. It was a street, you know, a few blocks, you know, with big buildings. And this is where people lived, in apartments. They were crowded, but you know. But there was no place.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had to go to the city government to get a [INAUDIBLE] register us and give us quarters that were helping out, you know, the refugees. They give us quarters. So they gave us a place.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, people lived-- in Germany, people lived, you know, almost like in America. They had an apartment. They had a dining room, a living room, a kitchen. And they had three bedrooms, nice apartment. It's a nice neighborhood, too, not in the camp, out in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time, the government were trying to help people, German people, which belong to-- to the Nazi party, to denazifizierung, you know, to-- to wash their hands from being Nazis. So they put in Jewish people, and moved in. And then they will try to live with the Jewish people. They should treat them nice, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how we got the little bedroom, you know. The kitchen was for everybody to use, you know, and the bathroom. And we lived in that bedroom. There was one double bed and a crib. There was a little oven, you know, to cook, and a table. That was-- that was the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a son, a child by then? You said you had a crib.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had already-- we had already a child. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your first child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: His name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bernie. That was Bernie, my first child. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] when we moved in there, my wife was pregnant. We didn't have the child yet. But she had him in Stuttgart. He was born in Stuttgart. But then we had to put in a crib, as-- as tight as it was, the room, you know. You had to find a corner, you know, for-- for a crib. It was a pride and joy, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed until-- I don't know the dates, but until '49. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We stayed until '49. And it's a good thing we didn't stay in the camp. Probably it was-- I don't know. Because when they-- in 1949, they liquidated the-- the camp in Stuttgart. They liquidated a [INAUDIBLE], a lot of DP camps, because they took people. They immigrated them. Some of them went to Israel. Some of them went to America.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, President Truman signed a-- a law that to allow refugees, not only Jews, refugees to come to America. You see? So there was a mass immigration at that time. And this is how they liquidated. They closed up those DP camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were living in a city, even though in-- in one room. But it was not forever. You always look forward, you know, to have a better life. Somewhere-- we didn't know where it was. But since it came out, the law, so we-- we registered to go to America. I wanted to go to Israel because I felt that the place for a Jew is Israel. You know, if we got already-- at that time was it was '48, you know. It was already officially, you know, Israel was a Jewish state. But my-- my father-in-law had cousins in-- in Chicago. So he said he's going to go because he's got family. And in my mind, I figured like this. I went through so much in life. All my youth, you know, is gone, you know. And I knew, because my cousin, Motek Rosenblatt, he was a Zionist before the war. So he went to Israel. And he wrote very bad letters. It was so hard in the beginning. It was very hard, very hard. It was hard with food, hard with jobs. Everything was hard, you know. So he-- he said, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE], hold off to come to Israel. If you can hold off until things will get probably better.

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't know. But right now, he says, it's not good. So that's why I was not rushing. You see? So in the meantime, there was a chance to go to America. So I figure I suffered so much, you know, in my life.

speaker: interviewee
text: In America, maybe I stand, you know, a better life. Because people were talking about America a lot, you know. There's opportunities. There's, you know, democracy. You know, so I figured, you know, maybe this is the place to go. And we decided to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '49. We came here at Thanksgiving. We had Thanksgiving turkey. We had on the boat because the boat came into Hudson Bay, you know. They didn't want to park the boat overnight in the holiday because it's very expensive to go into the harbor, you know, to anchor in the harbor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they stayed in the bay, you know. We saw all New York, the lights and everything. We had a good meal, a turkey meal. What was the name of your boat or ship that came over.

speaker: interviewee
text: General McRae. It was those transporters that were transporting GIs, you know, to overseas, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then after Thanksgiving you docked. And then where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: After Thanksgiving we-- I was supposed to go to Seattle, Washington. My contract was Seattle, Washington. So we figured-- you know, we started inquiring, where's Seattle, Washington? I look at the map. It was-- it's on the other end of America. In the European way of thinking, you know, we thought to go, like in Russia, to go far is Siberia already, though, on the other end of Russia, or on the--

speaker: interviewee
text: So we figured, you know, it wouldn't be such a good idea to go that far. Who knows? It might be not as good. And we came to New York. And my child was-- got sick, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we didn't know he ran a fever. So the doctor said to put us in a hotel where they had all the DPs. You know, it was 103 and Broadway, Royalton Hotel. So we stayed there-- I don't remember-- a week or two we stayed there. And then we had to go to Seattle. Our luggage went without us to Seattle so-- because that was the destination. In the meantime, our ticket was to go to Chicago. In Chicago, we had to disembark the train and wait for-- for the next train that goes to-- because just didn't go direct to Seattle.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the meantime, my-- my child was better. They gave him-- I don't know-- penicillin. And the fever when down. And then it went back up. So-- so they decided to put him in the hospital because I went to see at the station a doctor. So he said, I wouldn't-- I would put him in the hospital because he's tired. He told us, you are tired too. So maybe he should put us in a hotel and the child in-- in Mount Sinai Hospital. And then over there they found out that he's got measles. So he got measles. So they didn't have a department for measles. So they tried to push him away in another-- in another. So I was screaming at them. I said, what do you mean you don't have? It's a hospital, you know. He not going to stay here forever.

speaker: interviewee
text: See that he gets more or less well, and then you take him home. Because in Europe, hospital was a bad word because they didn't treat people good in a hospital. You know, people were dying in a hospital. So we liked to have everything at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Home care was better than a hospital. You see? So anyway, they kept him-- I don't know-- a week. Then we took him to the hotel, and he was all right. So we stayed in a hotel until they could find an apartment for us, because there are so many coming. So the joint, the Jewish family service, you know, couldn't find enough apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: In those days, apartment was a terrible thing to find. You had to-- you had to buy an apartment. So people were paying $500, $300 just to-- to the landlords. The landlords, you know, they tried to make hay in a case like this. So-- but the joint wouldn't give any money to the landlords. They said, if they have furniture, if they want to sell the furniture, we'll buy you the furniture, and that's fine. So we found an apartment where they had furniture, you know. I don't know remember how much-- $300. It was a lot of money at that time, you know, in 1949. So they gave the landlord $300, you know. And we moved in. It was a big, nice apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed with the in-laws until-- until we could find an apartment of our own. In the meantime, you know, I went to work. And I worked hard. I worked in a furniture factory, piece work. Did you understand English? Did you speak English at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I studied English before the war because my brother Henry had a girlfriend that went to Australia. And she promised him that she's going to take him to Australia, you know. So he was studying English by a teacher. And I-- when he did the homework, I had to help him because I had to listen, you know, if he knows the homework. So I learned a little bit. Then when I came to Germany, I had a teacher. I hired a teacher. And she taught me English. I had to teach her Russian. She wanted to learn Russian. But she didn't pay me. I had to pay her. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was all right. So I knew a little bit already. You see? So on the boat, I could get around a little bit better because I understood a little, and not-- not a lot. But it was very helpful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I came to-- to Chicago. You know, it was easier for me. I took a job. It was easier already. But piece work was very hard. It was very hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was a good worker. Because I was a good worker, they gave me a hard time. The foreman, he put me in a corner where all the steam pipes were going, you know, because they had to heat the clay, you know, because they were sticking the furniture together. So is it clay they call it? Glue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Glue. Glue. Yeah, the hot glue. So in the summers in Chicago, so hot. And I got weak probably. And my back gave in. So I got a back problem that I had to-- I had to go for an operation on the back because I could work. I couldn't walk anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- to Mayo Clinic. And they operated on my back. But ever since, I'm not-- I'm not the same anymore. And then after I came home after the operation, I got severe hay fever. This was in Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Chicago. And I couldn't stay in Chicago anymore. As I found out that Arizona and Colorado has got very little ragweed pollen. So Arizona was harder to-- to settle down with jobs or something. So I-- and I liked the mountains.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I settled over here. And that was very hard. It was hard to get a job for $0.75 an hour here. So did you move? Let me back up a little.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: You came by yourself. Now, your wife and-- and Bernie were in--

speaker: interviewee
text: The kids, I had two kids already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had another child. And his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teddy, Ted. You know, Ted got born-- was born in 1950.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you-- oh, and then you came--

speaker: interviewee
text: We were already in Chicago. So I had two-- two children. So with two children to move, and the recommendation was not the best to settle down and make a living in Colorado, especially in Denver. Denver had just a Gates factory and Samsonite. It used to be Shwayder's before it became Samsonite.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time when I came here, I came by myself. My wife wouldn't want to go. She had a mom and dad over there. So I said I'm not going to stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not going to, because I-- I saw people with asthma, with this and that. And I said, I'm not going to live like that, you know. So I left over here.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year did you come to Denver, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Denver in '50-- first time I came, '53. But I settled by myself in '55. I went back. And I-- I still couldn't stand it. You know, I was sick, you know. So I left for good over there by myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I-- somehow, you know, I made it through it. At first, I was taking a job as electrician, amateur binding. They paid very little, $0.75 an hour. And the guy was standing next to me. A guy, he was so fast. I said, how much do you make an hour? He said, $1.03. I said, oh, boy. With a family, how am I going to make a living here? So I was looking, you know, how to-- to get away from this kind of work, you know. So I find a job at the American novelty company. Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the Millers, Miller. His name was Paul Miller or something. So I worked over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just packing, you know, and sending away the packages. With a bad back, you know, after an operation, it was not a picnic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So It was tough and tough and tough, until I figured, you know, I have to find something better. So I inquired at the opportunity school to learn a barber. I wanted to become a-- a-- to-- to work with TV repair. So you had to have a background of electronics background.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't have it. So I decided I'm going to go to-- to barbering. So I took a few months until-- you know, you had to wait in a-- you know, until you could get in. And-- and the inquiry to fill out, it was a question, why do you want to be a barber? So I wrote in I was a barber in the old country. Because I was-- I was trying to hurry up. You had to go nine months, you know, eight hours a day to school. And here I have a wife and two kids, you know. How am I going to make a living? Nine months, you know, it's-- it's a long ways.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I wanted them to come. They were not here yet. But I wanted them to come as soon as possible. So good enough. And they called me. The instructor had an interview with me. He said, you were a barber in the old country. He said, you don't have to go nine months. Oh. The sun was started shining. I didn't have to go nine months.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, how long do I have to go? He said, you take a brush up course. I said, well, how long is a brush up course? He said, whenever you're ready, you take an exam.

speaker: interviewee
text: Whenever you're ready, even in two weeks, in three weeks, in one month, whenever. That was something, you know, to think about. But it was harder. I-- I was not a barber.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to start working at a chair, see all the other kids that were not barbers, you know, they ask the instructor how do you do this, how do you do that, how do you shave here, how do you shave there? I just knew how to hold a comb and a shear, how to hold it. I never cut hair with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I pushed it through. I looked for what he told-- what he tells other people how to do around the circle ear, which was hard. It's hard, especially when you don't know it. You're afraid you might cut off an ear. [LAUGHS] You know, they're not kidding. It's hard.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyway, I'm a perfectionist, you know. If I gave a haircut, it was-- it looked good. And he liked it, the instructor. After five weeks, he comes over to me. He says, Al, did you inquire for your examination? I said, oh, no. I think it's too early. Don't you think it's too early? He said, no, I think you're-- you're going to do all right. So I went to the examination.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they talked me into taking an apprentice examination, not the-- not the junior men, master-- master. Because as a master examination, I can open up my own shop. With apprentice, I have to work two years in a-- in a shop and then go to another examination for master barber. And then I can open up a shop.

speaker: interviewee
text: So two exams. So I figured maybe the-- the first exam is easy. So I'm going to go on a job. As long as I can make a living, you know, it's OK. And what do you think happened? I passed the exam, and I worked two years. And after two years was the same exam, exactly the same. But it was harder. Why? Because I forgot already a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: After two years, I forgot. [LAUGHS] But I-- I did all right. I went right away to a first-class shop on East Colfax. And I-- there was a guy also, a survivor from a concentration-- he was an excellent barber. And he wanted to open up a barber shop with me. You know, he didn't want to work for somebody else. He was a terrific barber. I don't know. He was a master of the trade, really. So, and we you decided-- you know, I borrowed a little money here and there, and he too, you know. We didn't have-- [LAUGHS] And we took on Cedar, on East Cedar and Holly, we took an empty store next to the J and M, an empty store. And we put in the fixtures, the chairs, and plumbing, you know, and we started working. And good enough. We had good clientele. We had a manicurist, a shoeshine. We opened up a first class shop with all the [INAUDIBLE]. [LAUGHS] And so I didn't stay with the barbering too long, but a couple years, you know. And then after one year, I went to the west side. I changed with another guy. You know, he took my part of the barber shop for his West Colfax.

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we'll stop this tape now.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
